chunk_summary: This section discusses specialist-led immunotherapy and biologic options for IgE-mediated food allergies and EoE, emphasizing counseling boundaries, patient selection, and the limitations of these therapies.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of immunotherapy in managing IgE-mediated food allergies, including its limitations and requirements.
Identify the appropriate use of biologic therapies, such as anti-IgE, in food allergy and EoE management.
Recognize the importance of specialist oversight and patient selection in immunotherapy and biologic treatments.
Evaluate the challenges of access, cost, and adherence in emerging therapies, particularly in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Immunotherapy approaches (oral, sublingual, epicutaneous) aim to raise reaction thresholds but do not cure food allergies.
Children on immunotherapy must continue allergen avoidance, regular dosing, and carry IM epinephrine due to breakthrough reaction risks.
Anti-IgE therapy is an adjunct or bridge in some immunotherapy protocols but offers incomplete and time-limited protection.
Biologic therapy for EoE is an option for refractory cases when diet and swallowed steroids fail, requiring endoscopic follow-up.
In India, biologics are tertiary-care options due to access and cost constraints and are not routine outpatient solutions.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: "Anti-IgE therapy can reduce the risk of reactions and is being used as an adjunct or bridge in some immunotherapy protocols, but it requires specialist oversight, monitoring, and clear communication that protection is incomplete and time-limited." (Source: Reference 2)
